raw_response:
# CC7.1 Compliance Assessment — Zenith Cloud Technologies
## SOC 2 Type II Audit | Q4 2025 Observation Period (October 1 – December 31, 2025)

---

## Preliminary Scope Note

The penetration test executive summary (PT-2025-ANNUAL-EXT) is **not directly relevant** to CC7.1, which concerns detection and monitoring procedures rather than offensive testing. It is noted for completeness but excluded from findings below. PT findings (IDOR in billing API, over-permissive Lambda IAM role) would be relevant to other controls (CC6.x, CC6.6) and are flagged separately to the engagement team.

---

## Findings Summary

| # | Title | Severity |
|---|-------|----------|
| F-01 | Unauthorized Scan Frequency Reduction During Code Freeze — No Formal Approval | **Critical** |
| F-02 | Detection Gap: Two Critical Vulnerabilities Undetected Due to Scan Cadence Reduction | **Critical** |
| F-03 | SLA Breach on VULN-8011 Without Active Risk Acceptance or Compensating Control Validation | **High** |
| F-04 | Scan Coverage Gap — Assets Consistently Below Expected Count Across All Tools | **High** |
| F-05 | Expired SLA Extension on VULN-8011 Not Renewed or Escalated | **High** |
| F-06 | Weekly Cadence Not Restored After Code Freeze Lift | **High** |
| F-07 | VULN-8021 Compensating Control Not Formally Documented as Risk Acceptance | **Medium** |
| F-08 | AWS Inspector Coverage Gap During Code Freeze Period | **Medium** |
| F-09 | Authentication Success Rate Below 100% With No Documented Remediation | **Medium** |
| F-10 | December Monthly SLA Compliance Report — Likely Not Generated Per Policy Requirements | **Medium** |
| F-11 | VULN-8024 D-Link NAS EoP: Compensating Control Referenced Without Formal Risk Acceptance | **Medium** |
| F-12 | EXC-2025-095 Dates Internally Inconsistent | **Low** |

---

## Detailed Findings

---

### F-01 | Unauthorized Scan Frequency Reduction During Code Freeze — No Formal Approval
**Severity: Critical**

**Evidence Sources:** Scan Execution Log (QSC-2025-049, QSC-2025-050, AWI-2025-049, AWI-2025-050); Vulnerability Management Policy §3.2, §8; Exception Register

**Observation:**

Qualys VMDR and AWS Inspector scans were reduced from weekly to biweekly cadence during the December 1–22 code freeze, creating a **14-day gap** between the November 30 scan (QSC-2025-048) and the December 14 scan (QSC-2025-049). The scan logs explicitly state this was done *"per Platform Engineering request to avoid scan-triggered alerts during freeze"* and was initiated as a **Manual Override** rather than through automated scheduling.

Policy §3.2 is unambiguous:

> *"Scan frequency must not be reduced below weekly under any circumstances including: Code freezes or change freezes."*

Policy §8 requires that any deviation from policy requires written request, risk assessment, CISO approval, and documentation in the exception register with a maximum 90-day duration.

The exception register contains **no entry** corresponding to this scan frequency reduction. EXC-2025-088 (the code freeze exception) addresses change moratorium only — it does not reference or authorize any modification to scanning frequency. The scan log itself acknowledges the absence of formal approval: *"No formal change request or CISO approval documented."*

This represents a direct, self-acknowledged violation of a named policy control that is also one of the most directly testable attributes under CC7.1. The policy was explicitly designed to prohibit exactly this scenario, yet the control was bypassed by an operational team (Platform Engineering) without security governance involvement.

**Why This Is a Control Gap:**

CC7.1 requires that detection and monitoring procedures be operative. A 14-day interruption in the primary infrastructure scanning tool — caused by an unauthorized configuration change — directly impairs the entity's ability to detect newly introduced vulnerabilities. The bypass was informal, undocumented at the governance level, and self-executed by the requesting party.

---

### F-02 | Detection Gap: Two Critical Vulnerabilities Undetected Due to Scan Cadence Reduction
**Severity: Critical**

**Evidence Sources:** Scan Execution Log (QSC-2025-050, AWI-2025-049); Vulnerability Management Policy §3.2, §4 (SLA for Critical: 14 days)

**Observation:**

The December 28 scan log (QSC-2025-050) explicitly notes:

> *"2 new Critical vulns discovered that were not detected during the Dec 7 gap."*

This is a direct, documented consequence of the F-01 finding. Because no scan was run on December 7 (the date that would have been the next weekly scan), these Critical vulnerabilities remained undetected from their introduction until December 28 — a **minimum undetected exposure window** that cannot be precisely bounded without knowing when the vulnerabilities were introduced, but which spans at least the December 7–28 period (21 days).

Additionally, AWI-2025-049 (December 14 AWS Inspector scan) discovered **1 new Critical vulnerability in a Lambda layer** that remained open as of the December 28 scan (AWI-2025-050 confirms it is still open). This Critical was detected only because AWS Inspector was scanned on December 14, but the 14-day gap still delayed detection by at least one week relative to the required weekly cadence.

**SLA Impact:** The Critical remediation SLA is 14 calendar days (Policy §4). For the two Qualys-identified Criticals first detected December 28, the SLA clock begins December 28, pushing remediation deadlines into mid-January 2026. However, the actual introduction date of these vulnerabilities is unknown — if introduced before December 14, the SLA may already be running against an undetected clock. This is not determinable from available evidence and represents an additional audit inquiry item.

**Why This Is a Control Gap:**

The undetected exposure window is a direct, documented operational failure of the detection program — not a theoretical risk. CC7.1 requires detection of newly discovered vulnerabilities; these two Criticals were not detected within the control's required timeframe due to the unauthorized scan reduction documented in F-01.

---

### F-03 | SLA Breach on VULN-8011 Without Active Risk Acceptance or Compensating Control Validation
**Severity: High**

**Evidence Sources:** Remediation SLA Spreadsheet (VULN-8011); Exception Register (EXC-2025-092); Vulnerability Management Policy §4, §5

**Observation:**

VULN-8011 (CVE-2024-53677, Apache Struts Path Traversal, Medium, CVSS 5.8) on `legacy-crm-02` is marked **SLA Breached** as of the audit period, with 12 days past the December 17 deadline. The spreadsheet notes: *"App retirement Q2 2026 — no risk acceptance."*

The exception register shows EXC-2025-092 provided a 30-day SLA extension expiring December 17, 2025. The review notes state: *"Extension expired December 17. Vulnerability remains open. No renewal or formal risk acceptance filed."*

This creates a compounding gap:

1. **The vulnerability is in active SLA breach** with no governing exception, risk acceptance, or remediation in place.
2. **No compensating control validation evidence exists for the current period.** EXC-2025-092 referenced WAF and network restriction as compensating controls, but those were associated with the now-expired exception. Policy §5.2 requires compensating controls to be validated every 90 days and at the time of risk acceptance approval. With the exception expired and no new risk acceptance filed, there is no current validation framework for these controls.
3. **The application retirement timeline (Q2 2026) does not constitute a risk acceptance.** Policy §5.1 requires formal CISO approval, a maximum 6-month duration, quarterly revalidation, and documented compensating control validation. None of these requirements are met by an informal reference to a retirement plan.

**Why This Is a Control Gap:**

An open SLA breach without a governing risk acceptance or active compensating control validation means the organization cannot demonstrate the vulnerability is being managed within its defined control framework. This is a direct gap in the remediation tracking program that underpins CC7.1.

---

### F-04 | Scan Coverage Gap — Assets Consistently Below Expected Count Across All Tools
**Severity: High**

**Evidence Sources:** Scan Execution Log (all scan records); Vulnerability Management Policy §3.3

**Observation:**

Policy §3.3 requires **100% subnet coverage** and states all assets in the CMDB must be scanned at least once per scan cycle. The scan logs consistently show actual assets scanned below the expected count across all three tools throughout Q4:

| Tool | Expected | Typical Actual | Shortfall |
|------|----------|----------------|-----------|
| Qualys VMDR | 1,495 | 1,482–1,492 | 3–13 assets per scan |
| AWS Inspector | 342–352 (growing) | 342–351 | 0–4 assets |
| CrowdStrike | 492 | 484–491 | 1–8 endpoints |

While individual scan notes attribute some shortfall to endpoints being offline (travel, holiday), **no scan in the entire Q4 period achieved 100% coverage** for Qualys VMDR. The VMDR shortfall is consistent (never zero gap) and the notes do not account for the gap in all cases.

For AWS Inspector, by the December scans, only 350 of 352 expected assets are scanned — a gap that persists across both December scans without explanation, suggesting possible asset registration or configuration issues rather than transient availability.

The policy's 100% coverage requirement has no stated exception for offline assets, and no documented process exists for rescanning assets that were offline during their scheduled scan cycle to ensure they are scanned at least once per period.

**Why This Is a Control Gap:**

Consistent sub-100% coverage means some assets may never receive a scan within a given cycle, leaving potential vulnerabilities undetected. The absence of a compensating procedure (e.g., triggered rescan when an offline asset reconnects) and the lack of any documented reconciliation process creates a persistent monitoring gap.

---

### F-05 | Expired SLA Extension on VULN-8011 Not Renewed or Escalated
**Severity: High**

**Evidence Sources:** Exception Register (EXC-2025-092); Remediation SLA Spreadsheet (VULN-8011); Vulnerability Management Policy §4.2, §5

**Observation:**

This finding is analytically distinct from F-03, which addresses the current breach state. This finding addresses the **process failure** that allowed the breach to occur without governance intervention.

EXC-2025-092 expired on December 17, 2025. The vulnerability remained open. The exception register notes acknowledge the expiration and absence of renewal. However, there is no evidence that:

- The Security Operations team issued an escalation upon expiration
- The automated SLA dashboard (referenced in Policy §4.1) generated an alert or escalation for the transition from "exception-covered" to "breached" status
- The Application Engineering manager (Tom Reeves) or asset owner was notified of the impending expiration in advance
- The CISO was notified upon breach per any escalation procedure

Policy §4.2 requires monthly SLA compliance reports that must include exceptions and risk acceptances. If the November report was generated, it should have flagged EXC-2025-092 as expiring December 17 with no remediation path. No evidence of this report or any action taken based on it is present in the provided evidence package.

**Why This Is a Control Gap:**

A vulnerability management program's effectiveness depends not only on tracking but on active governance of expiring exceptions. The absence of an escalation workflow for expiring exceptions means SLA breaches can occur silently, which is inconsistent with the monitoring and detection objectives of CC7.1.

---

### F-06 | Weekly Scan Cadence Not Restored After Code Freeze Lift
**Severity: High**

**Evidence Sources:** Scan Execution Log (QSC-2025-050, AWI-2025-050); Vulnerability Management Policy §3.2

**Observation:**

The code freeze ended **December 22, 2025**. The next Qualys VMDR scan (QSC-2025-050) ran **December 28**, which is within one week of the freeze lift and arguably consistent with weekly cadence. However, the scan notes explicitly state:

> *"Scan frequency not yet restored to weekly — weekly cadence to resume January 5, 2026."*

This means the scan on December 28 was still being operated as part of the biweekly schedule, not as a restoration of weekly scanning. The distinction matters: if the biweekly schedule was in effect from December 1, the December 28 scan falls on a biweekly cadence from December 14, not a restored weekly cadence from December 22. No weekly scan was run between December 22 (freeze lift) and December 31 (end of audit period), leaving **9 days without a scheduled weekly scan** after the justification for reduced frequency had expired.

The same pattern applies to AWS Inspector (AWI-2025-050).

**Why This Is a Control Gap:**

The code freeze — the stated (though unauthorized) justification for scan reduction — ended December 22. The obligation to restore weekly scanning was immediate upon freeze lift. The scan logs document that weekly cadence was not restored within the Q4 audit period. This extends the unauthorized deviation through the end of the observation period.

---

### F-07 | VULN-8021 Compensating Control Not Formally Documented as Risk Acceptance
**Severity: Medium**

**Evidence Sources:** Remediation SLA Spreadsheet (VULN-8021); Vulnerability Management Policy §5

**Observation:**

VULN-8021 (CVE-2025-21294, Digest Authentication RCE, Medium, CVSS 6.7) on `legacy-api-03` notes *"Network segmentation as compensating control"* in the remediation tracking spreadsheet. The vulnerability was detected November 15, 2025, and the SLA deadline is February 13, 2026 — it is within SLA.

However, the exception register contains **no corresponding risk acceptance entry** for VULN-8021. Policy §5 requires that compensating controls be formally documented through the risk acceptance process, including CISO approval, a maximum 6-month duration, quarterly revalidation, and independent validation by the Security Operations team.

A Jira ticket note is not a substitute for a formal risk acceptance. Furthermore, Policy §5.2 requires compensating control validation evidence to include: control description, validation method, test results, validator identity, and timestamp — none of which are present in the spreadsheet entry.

**Why This Is a Control Gap:**

Compensating controls that are informally noted in a tracking spreadsheet without formal risk acceptance documentation cannot be verified as operational, approved, or subject to revalidation. This undermines the integrity of the remediation program's compensating control framework.

---

### F-08 | AWS Inspector Coverage Gap During Code Freeze Period
**Severity: Medium**

**Evidence Sources:** Scan Execution Log (AWI-2025-049, AWI-2025-050)

**Observation:**

During the code freeze period, AWS Inspector scanned **348 of 352 expected assets** on December 14 (AWI-2025-049) and **350 of 352** on December 28 (AWI-2025-050). The shortfall of 2–4 assets persists across both December scans and is not explained in the notes. This is distinct from the Qualys coverage issue (F-04) because:

1. The gap **increased** relative to pre-freeze scans (which showed 0–1 asset gaps for AWS Inspector)
2. The same assets appear to be missing in both December scans (suggesting a structural issue rather than transient availability)
3. AWS Inspector is an agent-based/API-driven tool that does not depend on network reachability in the same way as Qualys — unexplained coverage gaps are more anomalous

This may indicate that new assets were added to the environment (cloud workloads are dynamic) that have not been registered with AWS Inspector, a misconfiguration in the Inspector scanning scope, or assets that were decommissioned in the CMDB count but not removed from expected counts.

**Why This Is a Control Gap:**

Unexplained and persistent asset gaps in cloud scanning — particularly during a period of reduced network scanning frequency — leave potential cloud vulnerabilities undetected without documented justification.

---

### F-09 | Authentication Success Rate Below 100% With No Documented Remediation
**Severity: Medium**

**Evidence Sources:** Scan Execution Log (all Qualys VMDR scans); Vulnerability Management Policy §3.4

**Observation:**

Policy §3.4 requires credentialed scanning for all infrastructure scans. The Qualys VMDR scans throughout Q4 consistently show authentication success rates of **97.4% – 98.4%**, meaning approximately **22–39 assets per scan** are being scanned without valid credentials. CrowdStrike endpoints show similar patterns (98.4%–99.8% agent-based coverage).

No scan record documents any follow-up action when authentication failures occur, no escalation procedure is referenced, and no evidence is provided that the failing assets were rescanned with valid credentials within the same scan cycle. The policy requires authenticated scanning but does not define an exception or remediation procedure for authentication failures.

Assets scanned without credentials receive a reduced-fidelity scan — network-level vulnerabilities may be detected, but local privilege escalation vulnerabilities, misconfigurations, and many CVEs that require authenticated enumeration will be missed.

**Why This Is a Control Gap:**

A consistent ~2% authentication failure rate across 1,495 assets means approximately 25–39 assets per scan receive incomplete vulnerability assessments. Without a documented follow-up procedure or evidence that failed authentications are investigated and resolved, these assets represent a persistent blind spot in the detection program.

---

### F-10 | December Monthly SLA Compliance Report — Likely Not Generated Per Policy Requirements
**Severity: Medium**

**Evidence Sources:** Vulnerability Management Policy §4.2, §7; Remediation SLA Spreadsheet

**Observation:**

Policy §4.2 requires monthly SLA compliance reports for all severity tiers, including SLA compliance rates, mean time to remediate, aging distribution, and exceptions/risk acceptances. Policy §7 requires a monthly vulnerability management dashboard to the Security Steering Committee.

No monthly SLA compliance report for **December 2025** has been provided in the evidence package. Given that:

- VULN-8011 entered SLA breach status on December 17
- The code freeze scan reduction occurred December 1–28 without formal approval
- Two new Critical vulnerabilities were discovered December 28

A December report would need to capture these significant events. The absence of this report from the evidence package — whether because it was not generated or was not provided — represents either a reporting failure or an evidence collection gap.

Additionally, the **November monthly report** is also absent from the evidence package. Given that EXC-2025-092 was expiring December 17, the November report should have flagged this for advance action.

**Why This Is a Control Gap:**

Monthly reporting is a detective control that enables timely identification of SLA trends, approaching exceptions, and emerging vulnerabilities. The absence of this evidence prevents verification that the reporting control operated effectively during Q4.

---

### F-11 | VULN-8024 D-Link NAS: Compensating Control Referenced Without Formal Risk Acceptance
**Severity: Medium**

**Evidence Sources:** Remediation SLA Spreadsheet (VULN-8024); Vulnerability Management Policy §5

**Observation:**

VULN-8024 (CVE-2024-10914, D-Link NAS Command Injection, Low, CVSS 3.6) on `nas-dev-02` was detected **August 25, 2025** — predating the Q4 audit period — with an SLA deadline of February 21, 2026. The notes reference *"Isolated VLAN — hardware replacement Q1 2026"* as the disposition.

This is the same pattern as VULN-8021 (F-07): a compensating control (VLAN isolation) is informally noted in the tracking spreadsheet without a corresponding formal risk acceptance in the exception register.

Additionally, this vulnerability was detected **prior to the Q4 audit period** (August 25), meaning it has been open for over 127 days as of the audit period start. While it remains within SLA, the absence of formal risk acceptance documentation for a control that has been operative for this duration suggests the compensating control documentation gap is systemic rather than isolated.

**Why This Is a Control Gap:**

Same rationale as F-07. Informal compensating control references in a tracking tool do not satisfy the formal risk acceptance requirements in Policy §5 and do not provide auditable evidence of independent validation.

---

### F-12 | EXC-2025-095 Dates Internally Inconsistent
**Severity: Low**

**Evidence Sources:** Exception Register (EXC-2025-095)

**Observation:**

EXC-2025-095 (Emergency Patch Deployment — CVE-2025-29824) contains the following date inconsistency:

- **Request date:** November 28, 2025
- **Approval date:** November 28, 2025
- **Expiration date:** November 30, 2025
- **Review notes:** *"Emergency patch deployed **October 25**. Post-patch rescan confirmed remediation **October 27**."*

The vulnerability (VULN-8019) was first detected **October 19, 2025**, remediated **October 28** per the SLA spreadsheet, and the exception was approved **November 28** — approximately one month after the patch was deployed.

The exception appears to have been **filed retroactively** after the emergency patch was already deployed. While the policy (§6.2, §6.3) permits emergency patching during code freezes, the exception register is intended to document the authorization before or contemporaneously with the action, not weeks later. The October dates in the review notes conflict with the November request and approval dates.

Furthermore, VULN-8019 is already marked as remediated in the SLA spreadsheet (remediated October 28), yet EXC-2025-095 was not filed until November 28. This raises questions about the integrity of the exception register as a contemporaneous control record.

**Why This Is a Control Gap:**

Retroactive exception filing undermines the governance value of the exception management process. An exception register that is updated after the fact cannot serve its function as a pre-authorization or contemporaneous control record. While the underlying patching activity was appropriate and timely, the documentation does not accurately represent the sequence of authorization and action.

---

## Summary Assessment

| Category | Assessment |
|----------|------------|
| Scan Execution — October through November | **Satisfactory** — weekly cadence maintained, authenticated scanning in operation, multi-tool coverage consistent with policy |
| Scan Execution — December | **Deficient** — unauthorized frequency reduction, self-acknowledged policy violation, extended through end of audit period |
| Remediation SLA Management | **Partially Satisfactory** — strong performance on Critical/High remediation; one active breach (VULN-8011) without governing documentation |
| Risk Acceptance and Compensating Controls | **Deficient** — multiple informal compensating control references without formal risk acceptances; one expired exception with active breach |
| Exception Management | **Partially Satisfactory** — most exceptions properly documented; one retroactive filing identified; no exception filed for the most significant deviation of the period |
| Reporting | **Insufficient Evidence** — no monthly SLA compliance reports provided for Q4 |

**Overall CC7.1 Readiness:** The organization demonstrates strong foundational vulnerability management capabilities and tooling. However, the unauthorized scan frequency reduction in December, the resulting documented detection gap for Critical vulnerabilities, and the absence of formal risk acceptance for open vulnerabilities with compensating controls represent material control gaps that would likely result in one or more **exceptions to the CC7.1 opinion** for the Q4 2025 observation period.
